# UC-21.6.13 · Slow Page Identification and Render Performance by Geography

## Description

Ranks site paths and countries where server-reported response times show high averages and especially bad ninety-fifth percentile latency, after filtering for paths with enough traffic volume to be statistically meaningful.

## Value

Merchandising and digital teams can prioritize fixes where slow pages actually hurt the most shoppers—before cart abandonment shows up in sales reports—by steering engineering hours toward specific regions, content types, or edge-cache misconfigurations.

## Implementation

Ingest combined access logs with numeric `response_time` in seconds (convert to milliseconds in the search). Enrich `clientip` with a published GeoIP lookup at search time or index time. Exclude bots using `http_user_agent` filters. Pair this panel with separate real user monitoring feeds when you need paint and layout timing beyond server headers.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Web logs with `uri_path` and `response_time` parsed; GeoIP fields present or a lookup from `clientip`.
• Index retention covering at least several weeks of traffic to compare weekdays versus weekends honestly.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Standardize time zones on web servers before forwarding logs. Normalize `response_time` units in props if some vhosts emit microseconds. Maintain a bots lookup to subtract crawlers when marketing runs heavy SEO audits.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL (tune percentile and hits filters for your storefront):

```spl
index=web sourcetype=access_combined
| eval response_ms=tonumber(response_time)*1000
| eval country=coalesce(clientip_country, geoip_country, "unknown")
| stats avg(response_ms) as avg_ms perc95(response_ms) as p95_ms count as hits by uri_path country
| where p95_ms > 3000 AND hits > 100
| sort - p95_ms
```

Understanding this SPL

**Slow Page Identification and Render Performance by Geography** — turns raw access lines into per-path, per-country latency distributions and filters on volume so only meaningful paths surface.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=web`, `access_combined` plus optional enrichment. **App/TA**: Splunk Web Analytics snippet or vanilla access logs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• `eval response_ms` ensures comparable units regardless of ingest convention.
• `coalesce` picks the best-available country dimension.
• `stats ... by uri_path country` computes average, tail latency, and sample size together.
• `where p95_ms > 3000 AND hits > 100` balances severity with confidence.

Step 3 — Validate
Spot-check flagged paths against your application performance tooling or CDN reports for matching regions and timestamps. Replay a handful of URIs via VPN exit nodes located in alerting countries.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Embed the panel in an ecommerce reliability dashboard beside error-rate and order-volume tiles. Tie alert routing to owners of the storefront template responsible for each URI prefix. Consider visualizations: Bar chart by country, Map overlay, Drilldown table linking to deployments. Pair with synthetic checks only as a confirming signal, never the sole proof.

## SPL

```spl
index=web sourcetype=access_combined
| eval response_ms=tonumber(response_time)*1000
| eval country=coalesce(clientip_country, geoip_country, "unknown")
| stats avg(response_ms) as avg_ms perc95(response_ms) as p95_ms count as hits by uri_path country
| where p95_ms > 3000 AND hits > 100
| sort - p95_ms
```

## Visualization

Bar chart (p95_ms by country), Table (uri_path, country, hits, p95_ms), Map (country shading by p95_ms).

## Known False Positives

Regions with aggressive mobile networks, single large media assets A/B tested in one country, marketing campaign landing pages with hero video, and synthetic monitoring injectors can create heavy tails without broad customer impact.
